title: The Jordaan
standfirst: Amsterdam's most beloved neighbourhood — narrow canals, 17th-century houses, hidden courtyards, and the kind of brown cafés where you lose track of an afternoon.
intro_html:
The Jordaan started as a mistake. When Amsterdam expanded its canal ring in the early 17th century, the city planners drew the new residential quarter to the west — narrow streets, dense housing, no grand waterways. It was built for workers: tanners, cloth-dyers, and craftsmen who couldn't afford the canal ring addresses being snapped up by Golden Age merchants.

It stayed working-class for three centuries. The Jordaan had a reputation for poverty, overcrowding, and radical politics well into the 20th century. Then, slowly, the artists arrived. Then the galleries. Then the boutiques. Today the Jordaan has some of the highest residential property prices in the Netherlands, and the canal houses that once housed ten people to a room are selling for millions.

This is the neighbourhood where Amsterdam feels most like itself: unhurried, slightly eccentric, genuinely beautiful. Give it at least a morning. Give it a full day if you can.

todos.title: Wander de 9 Straatjes
todos.description:
The '9 Streets' are a micro-neighborhood within the Jordaan, a network of narrow, picturesque lanes that cross the city's grandest canals. This is the heart of Amsterdam's independent boutique culture, where you'll find everything from vintage fashion and specialty bookshops to artisanal chocolate makers.

It is the perfect place for a slow afternoon of 'window shopping' and people-watching from a sidewalk cafe. Every street has its own character, but they all share the quintessential Jordaan charm—small-scale, historic, and effortlessly stylish. It is widely considered one of the most photogenic shopping districts in the world.

todos.title: Discover Hidden Hofjes
todos.description:
Tucked away behind unremarkable doors and narrow alleys are the Jordaan's 'hofjes'—historic almshouses with beautiful, tranquil inner courtyards. These were originally built by wealthy philanthropists to provide housing for elderly women and the poor, offering a peaceful sanctuary from the bustling city outside.

Wandering through these hidden gardens feels like stepping back into the 17th century. While many are still private residences, several are open to the respectful visitor. The Hofje van Brienen and the Karthuizerhof are two of the most spectacular, featuring ancient trees, meticulously manicured lawns, and a level of silence that is rare in the heart of Amsterdam.

todos.title: Noordermarkt
todos.description:
The Noordermarkt is one of Amsterdam's most beloved squares, transforming every Monday and Saturday into a bustling, eclectic market. On Saturdays, it hosts a vibrant organic farmers' market (the Boerenmarkt), where locals gather to buy artisanal cheeses, fresh flowers, and locally produced honey.

Housed in the shadow of the historic Noorderkerk church, the market feels like the true heart of the Jordaan. Even when the market isn't in session, the square is a perfect spot for people-watching from one of the many cafes—most notably Winkel 43, famous for serving what is widely considered the best apple pie in the city.

todos.title: Electric Ladyland
todos.description:
Electric Ladyland is the world's first museum of fluorescent art, a small but mind-bending gallery located in a basement in the Jordaan. This is not a passive museum; it is an 'interactive' experience where visitors are encouraged to become part of the art as they explore rooms filled with glowing rocks, phosphorescent sculptures, and psychedelic light installations.

It is a quirky, wonderful outlier in the Amsterdam museum scene, celebrating the beauty of light and color in a way that is both educational and deeply surreal. The owner's passion for the science of luminescence is infectious, making this one of the most unique and memorable stops in the entire neighborhood.

foods.title: Winkel 43
foods.description:
The 'appeltaart' at Winkel 43 is a local legend. For decades, this corner cafe on the Noordermarkt has been serving thick, warm wedges of apple pie topped with a massive dollop of freshly whipped cream. The crust is thick and cake-like, and the filling is packed with cinnamon-spiced apples.

On market days, the queue often stretches around the corner, but it moves quickly and the reward is worth every minute. Secure a spot on the sun-drenched terrace if you can, and enjoy what many consider to be the definitive Dutch culinary experience.

foods.title: Café 't Smalle
foods.description:
Operating out of a converted 18th-century jenever distillery on the Egelantiersgracht, 't Smalle is the quintessential Amsterdam 'brown cafe.' The interior is a symphony of dark wood, polished brass, and stained glass, illuminated by the soft glow of candles and vintage lamps.

In the summer, its tiny waterfront terrace is one of the most coveted spots in the city. It’s a place where time seems to slow down, perfect for a quiet afternoon jenever (Dutch gin) while watching the boats navigate the narrow, tree-lined canal just inches from your table.

foods.title: Waterkant
foods.description:
Located on the southern edge of the Jordaan under a multi-story parking garage, Waterkant is a Surinamese-inspired bar that has become a cult favorite among young Amsterdammers. The massive waterfront terrace is the draw, offering bright colors, loud music, and a tropical energy that is rare in the city center.

The menu features Surinamese classics like roti and baka bana (fried plantain), along with a great selection of local beers. It is the perfect place for a high-energy sunset drink, where you can dangle your legs over the canal wall and soak up the city's modern, multicultural vibe.

products.title: Jordaan Walking Tour
products.description: A 2-hour small-group guided walk through the nine streets, hidden hofjes, and canal-side architecture with a local guide who actually lives here.

products.title: Anne Frank House
products.description: The Jordaan's most significant site. Pre-booking is essential. Time slots release on the 1st of each month and sell out in hours.

getting_there.text: 20-minute walk from Amsterdam Centraal Station along the Singel canal.
